---
title: Tired old kitchen gets a face-lift
author: Jeffrey S. Racine
date: 2024-11-30
draft: false
feed: true
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

# Confession time

I have a confession to make - I suffer from emotional trauma caused by a
prior kitchen renovation. This arose from a questionable decision made
decades ago in order to save a few bucks when we had Home-Depot-designed
kitchen cabinets installed in our previous house in Syracuse NY (a 1910
Tudor-style home). The cabinet installer was a trade whose sales pitch
was "I will not accept a penny from you until the job is complete and
you are fully satisfied"). He was a repeat no-show who somehow managed
to damage 1 cabinet door and 5 drawers during the installation which
then impacted electricians, plumbers, and painters who were also
scheduled for the renovation. And after numerous sob stories as to why
he could not show up and complete the work as announced, he did manage
to show up unannounced on a Friday afternoon, reeking of alcohol, armed
with yet another sob story (this time that his house would be
repossessed unless he had cash by 5 pm, while his truck idled our
driveway with his girlfriend pressuring him and calling out of the truck
to "make them" pay). I also recall we said we would never ever
contemplate another kitchen renovation. Never. Ever.

I have another confession to make - I recently consented to proceed with
another kitchen renovation. And let me tell you, spending your kids'
inheritance on a kitchen renovation is taxing in more ways than one! You
have to deal with the mess, the noise, the dust, the inconvenience, the
cost, and the stress, not to mention the reawakening of the ghosts of
renovations past.

We bought our current home in 2005 (an 1816 Heritage home). The previous
owners had spruced up the kitchen to sell the house by installing maple
door cabinets and engineered veneer flooring layered on top of plywood
layered on top of the original wide plank pine flooring. The kitchen was
functional though not to our taste, but after two decades of daily use
it was tired, appliances were failing, and the cabinets were really
showing their age. In 2023 we realized that a full kitchen renovation
was inevitable and imminent, although the thought of washing our dishes
in our bathtub for months on end compounded by the memories of the
previous renovation almost stopped us from taking this one on.

So, in the spring of 2024, we settled on a contractor and cabinet maker
and planned to commence with demolition in the Fall of 2024. This time
we did our best to line up a contractor and established local trades
and, knock wood, things seem to be progressing much more smoothly than
before. Construction is expected to be finished by the New Year.

During this renovation I logged progress with photos, and decided to
share the story in this blog post. Contracts were signed, a general
timeline established, appliances ordered and certain up front payments
made, and renovations began within a few weeks of what we were told to
expect in the spring of 2024 (Sept. 26). The trades have been punctual,
personable, and professional, and the contractor overseeing the project
has been a joy to work with.

I think the pictures are pretty interesting, and the placement in each
block of photos that follow makes it easy to track each area from start
to finish. The scope of the project grew beyond just the kitchen, and we
took the kitchen, pantry/laundry, and mudroom down to the studs, or more
accurately down to the lathe-and-plaster walls and knob-and-tube wiring.
Note that, in the pictorial timeline below, the left top three pictures
are of the same three objects throughout, namely 1) the main kitchen, 2)
the pantry/laundry room, and 3) the mudroom, while other pics in each
block appear for posterity in no particular order.

# Pre-Renovation

The areas calling for attention were the main kitchen (top left below),
pantry/laundry off the main kitchen, and the mudroom hall off the main
kitchen. There was also a window hidden in the mudroom closet with
insulation showing on the outside (top right below). Counters were
unsightly, cabinet box edge laminate was coming off (bottom left below),
appliances were worn out, and the backsplash by the sink was studded
with calcified deposits while wooden window jambs were cracked and in
poor shape. But a promising feature was that the original flooring under
two layers of engineered flooring and plywood appeared to be in good
shape, fingers crossed (bottom right below).

::: {layout-col="4"}
![](images/pre/kitchen_full_view.jpg){width="150" fig-align="center"}
![](images/pre/pantry.jpg){width="150" fig-align="center"}
![](images/pre/mudroom.jpg){width="150" fig-align="center"}
![](images/pre/mudroom_window.jpg){width="150" fig-align="center"}

![](images/pre/cabinet_laminate.jpg){width="150"
fig-align="center"}![](images/pre/microwave.jpg){width="150"
fig-align="center"} ![](images/pre/backsplash.jpg){width="150"
fig-align="center"}![](images/pre/floor_layer.jpg){width="150"
fig-align="center"}
:::

# Demolition and Structural Repairs

The first step was to remove the old cabinets, countertops, and
appliances. The multiple layers of non-original flooring was removed
down to the original wide plank pine flooring that was covered in glue
and levelling concrete (top left below). The pantry/laundry area was
gutted, pantry wall removed, and the mudroom closet gutted, while the
exterior window behind the mudroom closet was removed and the exterior
wall expertly restored (top right below). Ceilings had been lowered and
a beam ran across the kitchen, while a box hid the stairs going upstairs
to the loft above the kitchen. The beam was removed, and the box was
torn down to reveal the stairs. The ceiling was raised to the original
height, a hidden LVM beam was added which replaced the previous
intrusive beam and the walls were insulated and drywalled. The old
retro-fit windows above the sink with view-blocking cross-bars were
replaced with larger locally manufactured single-pane windows that were
more in keeping with the original style of the house (no photo). The
floor joists in the pantry/laundry required support (the log
construction of parts of the house was sagging and in need of support)
so concrete footers were poured in the basement below and steel beams
were then installed using metal supporting posts (bottom right below).

::: {layout-col="4"}
![](images/demo/full_view.jpg){width="150"}
![](images/demo/pantry.jpg){width="150"}
![](images/demo/mudroom.jpg){width="150"}
![](images/demo/mudroom_window.jpg){width="150"}

![](images/demo/knob_tube.jpg){width="150"}
![](images/demo/beam.jpg){width="150"}
![](images/demo/subfloor.jpg){width="150"}
![](images/demo/support.jpg){width="150"}
:::

# Construction & Floors

We were really apprehensive about the floors, as we had no idea what we
would find under the layers of flooring. But fortunately the original
wide plank pine floors were in fairly good shape, though they were
covered in glue and concrete. They had to remove the glue and concrete,
then sand the floors down to the original wood. The floors were then
sealed with a neutral finish. The pantry/laundry area was reconfigured
to allow for a sink, countertop, and backsplash. The mudroom was
reconfigured to allow for a new and larger, more functional, closet. The
walls were insulated and drywalled, and the ceilings were raised to
their original height. The new windows were installed, and the original
floors were refinished.

::: {layout-col="4"}
![](images/during/kitchen_full_view.jpg){width="150"}
![](images/during/pantry.jpg){width="150"}
![](images/during/mudroom.jpg){width="150"}
![](images/during/floor.jpg){width="150"}
:::

# Cabinet and Closet Installation

With the demo, floor recovery and refinishing, and wall/ceiling
reconstruction complete, the refinished floors were covered in Ram board
and the Ram board covered with 1/4 inch plywood for protection, after
which kitchen, pantry, and mudroom cabinets and closets were installed.
The cabinets and closets were built by a local cabinet maker, and the
replacement windows above the sink were built locally with synthetic
frames to avoid water damage (the jamb will be quartz up to the window
for ease of cleaning and longevity, top right below). The pantry/laundry
area underwent the biggest change after removing a wall that hid the
narrow pantry shelving. In the pantry/laundry they installed floor to
ceiling cabinets, a sink and countertop/backsplash, and the old
side-by-side washer/dryer layout was reconfigured for stacked units and
moved. We are really happy that we decided to expand the scope of the
original project (originally confined to the kitchen only) to include
the pantry/laundry and mudroom closet. By tackling the pantry/laundry we
added two closets in the pantry that almost double the amount of closet
space originally confined to the mudroom only (two middle photos below).
This has created a cohesive and unified layout with almost double the
cabinet and closet space which was noticeably absent in the previous
configuration.

::: {layout-col="4"}
![](images/install/kitchen_full_view.jpg){width="150"
fig-align="center"} ![](images/install/pantry.jpg){width="150"
fig-align="center"} ![](images/install/mudroom.jpg){width="150"
fig-align="center"} ![](images/install/windows.jpg){width="150"
fig-align="center"}
:::

# Countertops, Painting, and Finishing Touches

To be continued... new solid wood doors installed with trim matching the
cabinet doors, plumbing, sinks, countertops, electrical, appliances...

# What We Appreciate...

The incredible crews who made this possible, the hidden surprises that
made it worthwhile, and not dealing with a list of "little things" that
no longer worked that rendered the old kitchen, pantry/laundry, and
mudroom a less-than-ideal space in which to function...

# What We Don't Miss...

Our dining room standing in for our old kitchen, our bathtub standing in
for our old dishwasher, and an induction pan and countertop mini oven
standing in for our old stove, flickering lights, a broken oven, manual
under-counter lights (no light switches), budget hollow bi-fold mudroom
closet doors that never closed properly, budget hollow doors on the
pantry/laundry/mudroom closet/kitchen bathroom, grey bare flooring, lack
of shelving in the pantry, lack of counter space, lack of electrical
outlets, low ceilings, stepping down off a threshold into the dining
room and pantry (due to engineered flooring on top of plywood on top of
the original floors)... I am sure I missed a few things but you get the
point...

::: {layout-col="4"}
![](images/fun/makeshift_kitchen.jpg){width="150"}
![](images/fun/bathtub.jpg){width="150"}
![](images/fun/hotdog.jpg){width="150"}
:::

# Thanks

We would like to thank not only the incredible talented people who made
this project blossom, but also our son who watched his future
inheritance being squandered yet encouraged us to proceed texting "Spend
as much as you like!! You’ve more than earned it, xoxo"
